conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me review the official eligibility rules so I can provide you with accurate guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, **do you have any children that you're responsible for?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have one child – his name's Alex and he's 8 years old. He lives with me here in Birmingham.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know about Alex.

Now, **are you currently living in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am – I live in Birmingham.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you for confirming.

Now, **is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for that information.

Just a few more questions to complete the assessment. **Does Alex have any special circumstances, such as:**
- Being in hospital or residential care for extended periods?
- Being in local authority care?
- Living with someone else (even temporarily)?

Or does he live with you full-time without any of these circumstances?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually I'm fostering Alex. He lives with me full-time at home, but I do get some support from the local authority to help with his maintenance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that important detail. This is very relevant to your eligibility.

**Can you tell me specifically what type of support you receive from the local authority? For example, do they pay anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they do – the council gives me money towards his accommodation and maintenance. It's part of the fostering arrangement.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official UK government rules I've reviewed, I can now provide you with a determination.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Result

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: While foster carers can receive Child Benefit for the children they foster, this is only possible when the local council is **not** paying anything towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Since you've confirmed that the council does provide you with money towards Alex's accommodation and maintenance as part of your fostering arrangement, you unfortunately don't qualify for Child Benefit for him.

The financial support you receive from the local authority for fostering Alex is separate from Child Benefit and is intended to cover his care costs. You cannot receive both types of support for the same child.

If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if the local authority support stops), you may wish to contact the Child Benefit Office to discuss whether you might then become eligible.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is an 8-year-old child who lives with the claimant in Birmingham, UK. The claimant is fostering Alex. According to the official eligibility rules, a person will get Child Benefit if they foster a child, BUT only as long as the local council is NOT paying anything towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. In this case, the claimant has confirmed that the local council does pay money towards Alex's accommodation and maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement. Therefore, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex due to the local authority financial support being provided.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering an 8-year-old child (Alex) and lives with him in Birmingham, UK. While foster carers can receive Child Benefit, this is only possible when the local council is not paying anything towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Since the claimant receives financial support from the local authority towards Alex's accommodation and maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement, they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

In this case, the claimant has confirmed that the local council does pay money towards Alex's accommodation and maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement. Therefore, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex due to the local authority financial support being provided.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering an 8-year-old child (Alex) and lives with him in Birmingham, UK. While foster carers can receive Child Benefit, this is only possible when the local council is not paying anything towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Since the claimant receives financial support from the local authority towards Alex's accommodation and maintenance as part of the fostering arrangement, they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
